title: Apply: The Close of the Convention
description: Apply your understanding of the close of the Constitutional Convention in this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: The Road to Reform, the Constitutional Convention, and the Battle for Ratification
The Close of the Convention: Benjamin Franklin's Closing Speech and the Dissenters
Apply: The Close of the Convention

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Benjamin Franklin’s closing speech at the Constitutional Convention.** 

Much of the Strength and Efficiency of any Government, in [[☃ definition 3]] & securing Happiness to the People depends on Opinion, on the general Opinion of the Goodness of that Government as well as of the Wisdom & Integrity of its Governors. I hope therefore that for our own Sakes, as a Part of the People, and for the Sake of our [[☃ definition 1]], we shall act heartily & unanimously in recommending this Constitution wherever our influence may extend and turn our future Thoughts and [[☃ definition 2]] to the Means of having it well administered. . . 

**Which statement best describes what Franklin would like the delegates to do?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Publicize their debates so the people will be informed
Encourage their states to ratify the Constitution
Insist upon a set of amendments to the Constitution
Point out what changes need to be made to the Constitution

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Benjamin Franklin’s closing speech at the Constitutional Convention.** 

Much of the Strength and Efficiency of any Government, in [[☃ definition 1]] & securing Happiness to the People depends on Opinion, on the general Opinion of the Goodness of that Government as well as of the Wisdom & Integrity of its Governors. I hope therefore that for our own Sakes, as a Part of the People, and for the Sake of our [[☃ definition 2]], we shall act heartily & unanimously in recommending this Constitution wherever our influence may extend and turn our future Thoughts and [[☃ definition 3]] to the Means of having it well administered. . . 

**Which of the following best explains the effect this speech had on the Constitutional Convention?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Few delegates signed the Constitution.
Half of the delegates signed the Constitution.
Most delegates signed the Constitution.
Every delegate signed the Constitution.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from Gouverneur Morris’s 1787 letter to George Washington, written one month after the Constitutional Convention ended. **

With Respect to this [[☃ definition 1]], I am far from being decided in my Opinion that they will consent [to ratify the Constitution]. True it is that the City and its neighbourhood are enthusiastic in the Cause: but I dread the cold and sour Temper of the back Counties, and still more the wicked [[☃ definition 5]] of those who have long [[☃ definition 2]] themselves to live on the Public, and cannot bear the Idea of being removed from the Power and Profit of State Government, which has been and still is the Means of supporting themselves, their Families and Dependents; And (which perhaps is more grateful) of [[☃ definition 3]] and humbling their political [[☃ definition 4]]. What opinions prevail more Southward I cannot guess. You are in Condition better than any other Person to judge of a great and important Part of that Country.

**Which statement best summarizes Morris’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He is unsure whether his state will ratify the Constitution because those in remote areas are more successful under a powerful state government.
He is certain that ratification will succeed because the wealthy people in cities support the Constitution’s new system of government.
He believes that ratification will fail because the new structure of government gives too much power to state governments.
He is unsure whether ratification will get enough support from the northern states, as the Constitution would require new taxes in cities.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from Gouverneur Morris’s 1787 letter to George Washington, written one month after the Constitutional Convention ended. **

With Respect to this [[☃ definition 1]], I am far from being decided in my Opinion that they will consent [to ratify the Constitution]. True it is that the City and its neighbourhood are enthusiastic in the Cause: but I dread the cold and sour Temper of the back Counties, and still more the wicked [[☃ definition 2]] of those who have long [[☃ definition 3]] themselves to live on the Public, and cannot bear the Idea of being removed from the Power and Profit of State Government, which has been and still is the Means of supporting themselves, their Families and Dependents; And (which perhaps is more grateful) of [[☃ definition 4]] and humbling their political [[☃ definition 5]]. What opinions prevail more Southward I cannot guess. You are in Condition better than any other Person to judge of a great and important Part of that Country.

**According to Morris, why might a person oppose the ratification of the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Constitution was contradictory, as too many people had a hand in creating the new system of government.
The Constitution benefited the people who lived in remote areas more than the people who lived in cities.
The Constitution did not address the root of the nation’s problems, as many people were living in poverty.
The Constitution took power from the state governments to create a stronger national government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Elbridge Gerry’s 1787 letter to the Massachusetts State Legislature.**

I have the honor to enclose, [[☃ definition 1]] to my commission, the Constitution proposed by the Federal Convention.

To this system I gave my dissent, and shall submit my objections to the honorable legislature.

It was painful for me, on a subject of such national importance, to differ from the respectable members who signed the Constitution; but [[☃ definition 2]], as I did, that the liberties of America were not secured by the system, it was my duty to oppose it. . . 

The Constitution proposed has few, if any, [[☃ definition 3]] features, but is rather a system of national government. Nevertheless, in many respects, I think it has great merit, and, by proper amendments, may be adapted to the “[[☃ definition 4]] of government, and preservation of liberty.”


**According to the excerpt, why did Gerry refuse to sign the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: relating to a system of government in which power is shared between states and a national government

choices.content: It did not create a strong enough national government.
It was too easy to add amendments, which would create confusion.
It did not include enough protections for individual freedoms.
It created a confederation where states held too much power.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Elbridge Gerry’s 1787 letter to the Massachusetts State Legislature.**

I have the honor to enclose, [[☃ definition 1]] to my commission, the Constitution proposed by the Federal Convention.

To this system I gave my dissent, and shall submit my objections to the honorable legislature.

It was painful for me, on a subject of such national importance, to differ from the respectable members who signed the Constitution; but [[☃ definition 2]], as I did, that the liberties of America were not secured by the system, it was my duty to oppose it. . . 

The Constitution proposed has few, if any, [[☃ definition 3]] features, but is rather a system of national government. Nevertheless, in many respects, I think it has great merit, and, by proper amendments, may be adapted to the “[[☃ definition 4]] of government, and preservation of liberty.”

**The objections Gerry describes in the passage were eventually addressed by which event?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: relating to a system of government in which power is shared between states and a national government

choices.content: The rise of political parties
The creation of a criminal court system
The passage of the Northwest Ordinance
The addition of the Bill of Rights

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from William Lenoir’s comments at the North Carolina ratifying convention.**

There was a very necessary clause in the Constitution, which is omitted in this [version]. That was a clause declaring that every power, &c., not given to Congress was reserved to the states. The omission of this clause makes the power [of the national government] so much greater. Men will naturally put the fullest construction on the power given them. Therefore lay all restraint on them, and form a plan to be understood by every gentleman of this committee and every individual of the community.

**Based on the passage, why did Lenoir object to the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It was not written in a way that every person could understand.
It did not include a clause that gave the states all powers that were not listed.
It gave too much power to individual citizens, rather than to the national government.
It would not prevent the rise of factions in government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from William Lenoir’s comments at the North Carolina ratifying convention.**

There was a very necessary clause in the Constitution, which is omitted in this [version]. That was a clause declaring that every power, &c., not given to Congress was reserved to the states. The omission of this clause makes the power [of the national government] so much greater. Men will naturally put the fullest construction on the power given them. Therefore lay all restraint on them, and form a plan to be understood by every gentleman of this committee and every individual of the community.

**Which framer would most likely agree with the ideas expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: George Mason
George Washington
Alexander Hamilton
Benjamin Franklin

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1787 article published in the *American Herald*, a Boston newspaper.**

The result of the Federal Convention has at length [[☃ definition 1]], after a profound secrecy being observed by the members who composed it . . . . This country, singular in every thing; in her rise, progress, extent of [[☃ definition 2]], in her emancipation and liberty, we flatter ourselves, is going to exhibit a new instance of a government being firmly and [[☃ definition 3]] established, without the arts, violences and bloodshed, which have disgraced the annals [dw: records] of the Eastern hemisphere.

Its acceptance will enroll the names of the Washingtons and Franklins, of the present age, with those of the [[☃ definition 4]] and [[☃ definition 5]] of antiquity. . . . The narrow, contracted politics, the [[☃ definition 6]] envy, the mean jealousy of little minds; the partial views, and the local prejudices, which have so long [delayed] the growth of this people, will be now annihilated. In their place, a more enlightened and [[☃ definition 7]] legislation, a more comprehensive wisdom, and a plain, manly system of national [[☃ definition 8]], will be happily substituted.

**Which statement best describes the author’s opinion of the Constitution?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: in such a way that it can’t be broken apart
one of the Seven Wise Men of Greece; credited with helping establish democracy
a political philosopher who ruled over Rome

choices.content: It is an example of a major political victory, in which a government peacefully changed for the better.
It represents an instance of tyranny, with a few wealthy individuals trying to seize power for themselves.
It accomplishes the goals of the Constitutional Convention; however, it will be successful only if the states can amend it.
It fails to adhere to the principles of democracy described in the works of ancient Greek thinkers like Solon and Numa.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “One of the People,” an article published in the *Massachusetts Centinel* in 1787.**

With you I admire both [Elbridge Gerry’s] virtues and his abilities;—but of you, I would wish to ask . . . whether when we cast our eyes over the list of the [[☃ definition 1]] members of [the] convention, we do not find many, very many characters, equal in every particular of both wisdom, genuine patriotism, honesty, and every other amiable accomplishment, with Mr. Gerry. To mention a WASHINGTON, a FRANKLIN, a MADISON, a KING and a GORHAM, I think sufficient: And whether, when we observe these worthies have to a man, set their names to, and risked their well established fame on the Federal Constitution, we have not from this circumstance in itself considered, as much reason to be prejudiced in favour of the federal system, and determine to give it our support, as to reject it on the ground of its not [[☃ definition 2]] in every particular with Mr. Gerry’s opinion. . . .

I say, when we consider these and many other particulars which must occur to every one who reflects on the subject; must we not conclude that the reasons in favor of the Constitution, as now proposed to the States, are much more [[☃ definition 3]] and conclusive than any which Mr. Gerry has to offer in favor of [[☃ definition 4]]? 


**Which statement best summarizes the author’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Citizens should refuse to support the Constitution because important figures like Washington, Franklin, and Madison have refused to endorse it.
Citizens should agree to support the Constitution only if the issues that Gerry has identified are addressed immediately.
Citizens should support the Constitution because those who have endorsed it are respected and their reasons for supporting it are persuasive.
Citizens should refuse to support the Constitution, as the framers who attended the Constitutional Convention cared only about their own fame.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1787 letter from Edmund Randolph to the Speaker of the Virginia House of Delegates.**

It is said in the resolutions which accompany the constitution, that it is to be submitted to a convention of delegates chosen in each State by the people thereof, for their [[☃ definition 1]] and ratification. The meaning of these terms is allowed universally to be, that the convention must either adopt the constitution in the whole, or reject it in the whole, and is positively forbidden to amend. If therefore, I had signed, I should have felt myself bound to be silent as to amendments, and to [[☃ definition 2]] to support the constitution without the correction of a letter. With this consequence before my eyes, and with a determination to attempt an amendment, I was taught by a regard for consistency not to sign.


**Which statement best summarizes Randolph’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He agreed to sign the Constitution, as he knew that there would be a second convention to adopt necessary amendments.
He refused to sign, as the Constitution did not include a process for how future generations could suggest amendments.
He agreed to sign the Constitution, even without necessary amendments, to keep the thirteen states under one central government.
He refused to sign because the ratifying conventions could not suggest necessary changes to the Constitution.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a 1787 letter from Edmund Randolph to the Speaker of the Virginia House of Delegates.**

It is said in the resolutions which accompany the constitution, that it is to be submitted to a convention of delegates chosen in each State by the people thereof, for their [[☃ definition 1]] and ratification. The meaning of these terms is allowed universally to be, that the convention must either adopt the constitution in the whole, or reject it in the whole, and is positively forbidden to amend. If therefore, I had signed, I should have felt myself bound to be silent as to amendments, and to [[☃ definition 2]] to support the constitution without the correction of a letter. With this consequence before my eyes, and with a determination to attempt an amendment, I was taught by a regard for consistency not to sign.


**Which other framer of the U.S. Constitution would likely agree with Randolph’s reasoning in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: James Madison
George Mason
Benjamin Franklin
George Washington
